sentences.text: We resumed a regular life.
I was working at my father's factory.
We opened up again.
My little brothers and sister continued on.
They went to cheder.
And my mother resumed cooking at our home.
I suppose by that time, these Nazis knew what was awaiting us at the end.
So we went back, And we lived like that for about a year.
And the rules [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were being made constantly diminishing our rights.
And then this went on until 1943.
The beginning of 1944.
They formed a ghetto in the city of Munkacs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember the downtrodden area, the worst area, encompassed certain [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] blocks, and that was designated to be the ghetto.
All Jews who lived outside the ghetto had to abandon their homes.
And all non-Jews who lived in the area have to leave.
And there were more Jewish people living outside the ghetto had to cram into this ghetto.
And I remember in our house, we had my mother's sister, whose husband was taken away.
And she had two children and some other cousins, and we were just crammed in.
We happened to live in the ghetto area, and they formed what they referred to the Judenrat, the elders of our community who were in touch with these Nazis.
And they had to have meetings, and these Nazis were giving them orders.
And all of these orders had to be carried out.
Many times the Judenrat, the elders, came back with bloody noses, beat up with blood all over them.
And anyone who didn't carry out, they did away with the families.
The families just disappear.
They instilled such fear into us.
We lived.
And then they brought in, from the neighboring villages they brought in Jews.
And we lived in miserable conditions.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You could go out during the day, but there was a curfew.
You had to be back by six o'clock.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father fended for the family.
We all did a part, and we all lived in real fear.
And then [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember listening to people, and they said it's 1944.
The Nazis are losing the war.
Maybe something is going to happen.
Maybe they'll stop.
Everyone lived with hope.
We had to hope.
That's part of life.
And then word came that we were going to be resettled in the Hungarian prairie.
We would be put to work to help the war effort.

And to start preparing for evacuation, I remember my mother baking cookies, and we went through rehearsals, how many pair of pants we can put on, how we will take [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the necessary things along.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Everything was lured into-- you know, we-- I can't think of the word-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we went as far as preparing cooking utensils and beddings to take along.
I mean, their deception were such that to keep us calm, they did almost anything and everything.
And I remember we didn't know when, but they said-- and I remember in May of 1944, we were awakened to gunshots and hollering and yelling.
And everybody out, and line up in the middle of the street.
And they were whipping people.
And I remember people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] crying and praying.
And they were cutting beards, and there was blood splattered all over.
I remember my mother threw a sheet down, and we threw stuff into the sheet.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] people holding on to their loved ones.

And we were in the middle of the street with blood strewn all over. | remember going through the streets to watch the brick factory, and non-Jews looking out the window, some people we knew, and nobody lifted a finger to help us.
I remember the pious Jews praying, you know, the [YIDDISH], the redemption will come.
God will help.
I remember at the time I sort of lost faith in God for the time being.
Our destination was to a brick factory where the setting was just right.
They had these sheds, long sheds, where they dried the bricks.
And there was our housing.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We had to do calisthenics.
My father had to go out in the middle of the brick factory.
They had to do all kinds of heavy calisthenics without their hat on.
My father was completely bald.
After a couple days, my father's eyes were puffed from sunstroke.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt sorry for my dad.
He couldn't do anything for the family.
They didn't feed us.
We had to carry brick from one end of the factory to the other for no reason whatsoever, just to torture us.
They didn't feed us.
And the setting was so where they had these railroad tracks come in, and they backed in these freight cars, these cattle cars.
And I remember people actually were packing up and they were standing in line to go, because they probably thought nothing can be as bad as we have it in the brick factory.
So people lined up.
And slowly, slowly they are emptying the brick factory.
My father, I remember, said, let's wait for the last transport.
We waited.
I remember we helped our grandfather, who was about 90 years old, and my grandmother who lived nothing but for [NON-ENGLISH].
And we were crammed into this freight car, about 80, 90 of us, I don't know how many.
There was just enough room to maybe sit down on the bundles.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In the rush we didn't take water.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in there for about three or four days and nights, I don't remember.
No bathroom facilities.
We had to use a corner.
The children were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] demanding water, the sick dying.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Anyway, we were there for about three, four days.
And we were to go to this Hungarian flatland.
Right.
If I remember, it was the Kalush brick factory.
Kalush.
The Munkacs ghetto.
Well, my father knew people on the Judenrat.
Everybody knew everybody, practically, and my father knew the people on the Judenrat.
And they would come in and they would discuss things.
But you always had hope, though.
My father went back.
Oh, my father-- I forgot to mention that during the last two years in 1942, all Jewish businesses had to take in a non-Jew as a partner.
So my father had a partner.
But my father, since it was his business before, his services were invaluable, just like anybody else.
See, there was a method to their madness, because they knew that eventually everybody is going to lose out on their businesses, and somebody will be ready to take over.
So my father had someone else as a partner.
And during the ghetto, while we were in the ghetto, my father went out and he worked.
Yes, he worked.
He worked.
So we did have-- since my father was not a businessman, you know, he was a worker and so on, I mean, his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] service was more valuable.
And they regarded that as a very important, whatever people could contribute.
In a way, which same thing happened when eventually we ended up in the concentration camps.
Right.
We had a little extra because all these people who would come in to have work done and repair, furniture repaired and work and so on.
So there were some bartering, a lot of bartering going on.
Instead of taking money, we took in food.
So my father was able to bring some food in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to the ghetto.
They woke us up that morning, and we were herded onto the streets.
And we had to line up with guards on both sides.
And we were herded into the brick factory.
Yes.
Right.
We walked.
It was on the outskirts of the city.
For about-- I would say we were there for about maybe a week to 10 days.
Yeah.
See, from the ghetto, there were no railroad tracks, so they couldn't put us on the railroad tracks.
So what they did is they took us to the outskirts of the city, and they placed us in the ghetto where we lived on the rations that we took along, because they didn't feed us there.
People were starving.
The ghetto in Munkacs wasn't as bad as the Warsaw ghetto.
The Warsaw ghetto, you know, went on for much, much longer, I don't know how long.
And people were starving there.
And it was the only way, you know, that we know having read books and listened to eyewitnesses, they went through sewers and so on to get food stuff.
See, with us it wasn't as strict.
And it wasn't as long.
Everything was already prearranged, pre-planned.
So they were much more efficient.
And that's another thing, is that the-- I don't know.
The outside world must have known of these things.
They could have at least bombed the railroad tracks to slow down their efficiency.
Maybe we would have been saved.
But anyway--
Yes, everything.
You know, we were-- we had it so bad that when they backed into cattle cars and they told us that we were going to the cattle cars.
They were going to take us to central Hungary to the plains and where they're going to put us to work.
So people stood in line.
They wanted to go because they wanted to go to work.
They felt they were going to give them food.
They're going to give them some place to sleep.
And they would be in the close quarters and so on.
So they went.
And while they were in the cattle cars for three, four days, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we ended up not in the Hungarian flats.
When they opened up the gates and we disembarked, it was Birkenau, Auschwitz, the death camps.
When they opened up those doors, did you know where you were?
No.
Nobody knew.
Nobody knew.
We didn't know where we were.
We traveled day and night with no windows.
There were just a little opening with barbed wire.
We didn't know where we were.
And, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] well, in the ghetto, they came around, and they said that before they take us away that everybody should give up their jewelry.
And I remember my mother had diamond earrings, and she took them off and she gave them to them.
Otherwise they would have probably been yanked off anyway.
And I remember the train stopped on the border someplace.
We didn't know where we were.

And they opened up-- I think they gave us some water, and they said, anybody else has got jewelry, you better give it up because it's going to be confiscated and you'll have to pay a heavy penalty if they find it on you.
And some people gave up some jewelry.
Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] what I heard since then that military trains had the priority many times.
You know, these transports, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they derailed them to a side track, and they sometimes waited a week on the side track before they continued on to Birkenau, Auschwitz.
And when they opened up these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cattle cars, half of the people were dead.
Anyway, we arrived.
And I remember they started yelling, [GERMAN]!
Get out!
Leave all your belongings behind.
It's going to follow you.
Stand in line.
All able-bodied men to the left.
All women and children [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the right.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there was the people with striped garb.
We didn't know who they were or where we were.
They kept saying [GERMAN]!
Go to work!
My father understood.
I was 14 and a half years old.
He told me to stand tall and lie about my age, tell them I'm 18.
I had my brother, who was younger.
And he had this little bag of food.
And by that time my mother and my little brothers and my sister and my aunt and my cousins went to the right.
And my father told my brother to take over the food stuff to my mother because we didn't know how long before we'll see one another again.
And they wouldn't let my little brother back.
And then all these people who were on the right, they marched them off to this building.
I remember there was some stench in the air.
There was a big chimney.
We didn't know anything.
And we know now these people were marched to these buildings.
They were asked to undress, to put their clothes neatly, and they were [GERMAN], disinfection.
And they were ushered into this room where there were looked like showers.
Now we know instead of showers they turned on these Zyklon B gas faucets and they gassed these poor souls.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the kind of death my family met.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After that the Sonderkommando [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went in there after they aired out with gas masks.
They unhooked these bodies.
Even in death you could tell family, family members.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went back in 1990 with fellow survivors with mixed emotions [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to pay my respect to my family.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I have no grave site.
And I remember these bombed out crematoriums.
The Nazis bombed them out because they didn't want to leave any traces of evidence.
I remember they had this guide tell tourists that Auschwitz, Birkenau, had the capacity of gassing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 20,000 people a day, and burning in the crematorium 10,000 people a day.
Therefore, they build these piles of wood where they burned people.
They reduced them to nothing but ashes.
At the time we didn't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So when I went back [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said Kaddish, the prayer for the dead.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
While my brother and my dad, we were ushered the other way on the double.
We ran to these barracks following orders, hoping, you know, to see the family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to undress.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They sheared our hair from all over our body.
I was just a kid.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So they sheared my head.
And we had to take a shower.
All we could hold on is our shoes and our belts.
They took all of our clothing away.
We were naked.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember I held onto pictures of my family I dared take along.
While in the shower I put them inside my shoe.
Then we had to line up and they tattooed us.
My brother has got A9327.
I have A9328.
My father was tattooed A9329.
They did it with pen and ink.
It hurt.
Our arms got swollen.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Anyone who cried [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] or screamed was beaten.
We had to endure.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were branded.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No longer David Mandel.
I was reduced to a number.
They didn't feed us.
I remember after a day or so they gave us a bowl of soup and it stunk.
There were some fellow prisoners.
We were separated through barbed wire.
They extended their skinny arms.
They wanted that soup, and they ate it.
And we couldn't understand how anybody can eat anything like that that has that foul smell.
Later on we were given the soup.
We were looking for solid things like potato peel, grassy stuff, whatever.
And later on we were so hungry, that soup was the most delicious thing we've ever had.
We were there for a few days.
Then they marched us off from Birkenau to Auschwitz, which is about, maybe, three kilometers.
I remember going through [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this gate.
It said 'Arbeit macht frei,' work makes you free.
Another lure to keep us calm.
We were there.
I ran into my uncle, who lived in Belgium with my mother's sister, who married my mother's sister.
And he was incarcerated for about two years.
The first time he heard news about the family.
And we were there.
My father was pulled out.
We had to line up one day.
But the second or third day my father was pulled out as a strong man.
Was put on a truck and carted off.
And my brother and I were left there.
My brother was 17.
I was 15 years old.
And we were just a couple of skinny kids.
My brother found a piece of moldy bread and ate it and got violently ill.
They took him to a Krankenbau, which is a hospital-like.
Meantime, I was alone while he was in the Krankenbau.
A truck came.
They put the remaining group onto a truck, and they carted me off to a nearby satellite camp of Auschwitz near Krakow called [INAUDIBLE].
So I was 15 years old, not quite 15 years old, all by myself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was on a detail.
I worked in a power plant.
I was unloading brick.
I remember we had a line.
We had to hand four bricks at a time like a chain gang.
The brick was like sandpaper.
It wore off-- the tips of my finger became nothing but flesh.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had to work I don't know how many hours a day.
I worked there for awhile, and then I worked in a coal mine.
The rations were starving rations.
Very few people survived three months.
Within three months they dwindled down to nothing but skin and bone.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had to stand in line for selection.
And we had to run through naked through the Nazis and overseers.
And they took our numbers.
All those whose numbers were taken [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were selected.
They would put them on a truck.
They were carted back to Auschwitz.
And they were in--
